vsans_citation > Overview / Citation Requirements / Editorial Review

# VSANS Instrument Publications

# VSANS Instrument Publications

If you have written a paper that reports measurements using this instrument and you do not see it in the list below, or if the paper is listed but the information is incorrect or out of date, please inform [contact details omitted].

## Citation Requirements

If you write a paper or give a presentation that reports measurements using the vSANS Instrument, please ensure the following:

*   **Acknowledgements:** Include an acknowledgement to the NSF, and to NIST if there are no NIST coauthors.
*   **Instrument Reference:** Include a reference to the paper describing the vSANS Instrument: 
    *   "The very small angle neutron scattering instrument at the National Institute of Standards and Technology", Barker J, et al. *J Appl. Cryst.* 55 (2) 271 (2022).
*   **Data Reduction Reference:** If you reduced and/or analyzed your data using the Igor Macros, include the reference:
    *   "Reduction and Analysis of SANS and USANS Data using Igor Pro", Kline, S. R. *J Appl. Cryst.* 39 (6), 895 (2006).
*   **Analysis Software:** If you analyzed your data using the SasView application, please follow the guidance outlined on the SasView publications site.

## Editorial Review
All papers with NIST coauthors must be reviewed and approved by the Washington Editorial Review Board (WERB). WERB reviews focus on:
1. Measurement uncertainties
2. Use of SI units
3. Brand names

sasview_user_documentation > Overview / Main documentation sections / Navigation links preserved from source / User note / Copyright and build information

# SasView User Documentation

# SasView User Documentation

SasView 6.1.3 provides user documentation for small-angle scattering analysis workflows, including model documentation, menus, fitting and other analyses, utilities, and practical usage guidance.

> Source note: the original page is a Sphinx documentation index page. It contains minimal content and primarily points users to the major sections of the SasView user manual.

## Main documentation sections

- [Model Documentation](https://www.sasview.org/docs/user/qtgui/Perspectives/Fitting/models/index.html) — model functions used in SasView fitting workflows.
- [Menu Bar](https://www.sasview.org/docs/user/menu_bar.html) — reference for application menu commands.
- [Fitting & Other Analyses](https://www.sasview.org/docs/user/analysis.html) — analysis workflows available in the SasView interface.
- [Tools & Utilities](https://www.sasview.org/docs/user/tools.html) — supporting utilities and tools.
- [Working with SasView](https://www.sasview.org/docs/user/working.html) — practical guidance for using SasView.

## Navigation links preserved from source

- [SasView Documentation Index](https://www.sasview.org/docs/index.html)
- [General Index](https://www.sasview.org/docs/genindex.html)
- [Python Module Index](https://www.sasview.org/docs/py-modindex.html)
- [Search](https://www.sasview.org/docs/search.html)
- [Page source](https://www.sasview.org/docs/_sources/user/user.rst.txt)

## User note

The source page notes that, on Windows, users can press **Alt + Left Arrow** to return to the previous page.

## Copyright and build information

- Copyright: © 2026, The SasView Project.
- Documentation generator: Sphinx 8.2.3.

text:
vsans_igor_reduction > Overview / Overview / Data Reduction using Macros built for Igor Pro

# SANS and USANS Data Reduction and Analysis Software

# SANS and USANS Data Reduction and Analysis Software

> **Status note:** This page is no longer being updated and the information may be out of date.

## Overview

## Data Reduction using Macros built for Igor Pro

SANS and USANS Data Reduction and Analysis software, written by [Steve Kline](https://www.nist.gov/people/steven-r-kline), is provided by NIST Center for Neutron Research in a visual and user-friendly form. The NCNR software is compatible with Macintosh/PC platforms and requires IGOR Pro be installed. You do not have to purchase IGOR Pro - a free demo version of IGOR Pro is available, and will produce ASCII output that can easily be read in by other graphics or analysis software packages. IGOR Pro is a commercial software product available to Mac/PC users.

**Latest Release: [NCNR_SANS_package](https://github.com/sansigormacros/ncnrsansigormacros/releases/latest)**

An upgrade of the reduction and analysis software has been completed based on user suggestions. These changes enhance the tools available to our users in order to plan better experiments, and permit faster, more complex analyses of their scattering data than are currently possible.

- All of the SANS and USANS Reduction and Analysis procedures are now in a single download package that includes an installer and updater.
- Installation package: [Latest GitHub release](https://github.com/sansigormacros/ncnrsansigormacros/releases/latest)
- Installation instructions: [Install_Instructions.txt](https://www.nist.gov/document/sans-igor-macros-installation-instructions)
- How to install the SANS Package: [SANS Reduction Macros: Installation Instructions](https://www.nist.gov/video/sans-reduction-macros-installation-instructions)
- A Quick Start guide to the package is highlighted in [video documentation](https://www.nist.gov/ncnr/sans-usans-reduction-video-tutorials)


- [What's New?](https://www.nist.gov/document/sans-igor-macros-new-features)
- Manuals are included as help files in the download package, and are available separately on Github as PDFs:
  - [SANS Reduction Help File](https://github.com/sansigormacros/docs/blob/main/SANS_Reduction_Help.pdf) (PDF)
  - [USANS Reduction Help File](https://github.com/sansigormacros/docs/blob/main/USANS_Reduction_Help.pdf) (PDF)
  - [VSANS Reduction Help File](https://github.com/sansigormacros/docs/blob/main/VSANS_Reduction_Help.pdf) (PDF)
    - [VSANS Step-by-Step Reduction Guide](https://www.nist.gov/document/vsans-reduction-step-step-guide) (PDF)
  - [Data Analysis Help File](https://github.com/sansigormacros/docs/blob/main/SANS_Analysis_Docs.pdf) (PDF)
  - [Model Functions Help File](https://github.com/sansigormacros/docs/blob/main/SANS_Model_Docs.pdf) (PDF)
  - [Simulation Help File](https://github.com/sansigormacros/docs/blob/main/Scattering_Simulation.pdf) (PDF)
  - [Polarized Beam Reduction](https://github.com/sansigormacros/docs/blob/main/Polarization_Reduction.pdf) (PDF)
  - [Real Space Modeling](https://github.com/sansigormacros/docs/blob/main/Real_SpaceModeling_Help.pdf) (PDF)
  - [2-D Resolution](https://github.com/sansigormacros/docs/blob/main/SANS_2D_Resolution.pdf) (PDF)
- Some of the new features in the package are highlighted in [video documentation](https://www.nist.gov/ncnr/sans-usans-reduction-video-tutorials)
- IGOR Pro and the free demo version of Igor are available from [WaveMetrics](http://www.wavemetrics.com/).

text:
vsans_igor_reduction > Data Analysis using SasView / Contacts / Source metadata

# SANS and USANS Data Reduction and Analysis Software

## Data Analysis using SasView

The recommended tool for analyzing small-angle scattering data is [SasView](http://www.sasview.org/). SasView was originally developed as part of the DANSE project funded by the US NSF under Award DMR-0520547, but is currently maintained by a collaboration between UTK, UMD, NIST, ORNL, ISIS, ESS, ILL, ANSTO, TU Delft, DLS, BAM and the scattering community. SasView also contains code developed with funding from the EU Horizon 2020 programme under the SINE2020 project (Grant No 654000). A list of all individual contributors can be found on the [SasView website](http://www.sasview.org/people/).

The NCNR Igor Pro Macros feature an analysis package, but the analysis portion is no longer supported or maintained.

## Contacts

The source page lists current support contacts for the NCNR Igor Macros. Direct names, email addresses, and phone numbers were omitted from this cleaned copy; refer to the live source page for current contact details.

## Source metadata

- Canonical source: https://www.nist.gov/ncnr/neutron-instruments/data-reduction-analysis/sans-software
- Created: 2019-01-29
- Last updated: 2026-05-27
- Page status: no longer being updated; information may be out of date.

Core data-reduction/software guidance, release/documentation links, status warning, and SasView analysis note preserved. Output filename follows the uploaded source filename stem: vsans_igor_reduction.html -> vsans_igor_reduction.md. -->

# VSANS Reduction Tutorial

# VSANS Reduction Tutorial

## Purpose

This page is a NIST remote-video entry for the **VSANS Reduction Tutorial**. It provides an embedded YouTube tutorial for reducing VSANS data and links the video to the broader SANS/USANS reduction tutorial collection.

## Video

- **Video title:** YouTube | NCNR VSANS Data Reduction Tutorial
- **Video URL:** [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_SB2gDxpwcI](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_SB2gDxpwcI)
- **YouTube video ID:** `_SB2gDxpwcI`
- **NIST canonical page:** [https://www.nist.gov/video/vsans-reduction-tutorial](https://www.nist.gov/video/vsans-reduction-tutorial)
- **NIST shortlink:** [https://www.nist.gov/media/604691](https://www.nist.gov/media/604691)
- **NIST search bundle/type:** `remote_video`
- **Embedded iframe source in saved HTML:** `vsans_reduction_video_example_files/_SB2gDxpwcI.htm`

## Appears in

- [SANS & USANS Reduction Video Tutorials](https://www.nist.gov/ncnr/sans-usans-reduction-video-tutorials)

## Source-cleaning notes

Navigation menus, U.S. government banner, NIST header/footer, search form, sharing widgets, feedback form, analytics, New Relic / Google Tag Manager / Cloudflare scripts, CSS, SVG icon markup, duplicate page wrappers, and back-to-top UI were removed. The video title, canonical NIST URL, YouTube link, iframe title/source, NIST media shortlink, and collection link were preserved.

text:
iucr_the_very_small_angle_neutron_scattering_instrument_at_the_national_institute_of_standards_and_technology > Overview / Instrument Overview # The very small angle neutron scattering instrument at the National Institute of Standards and Technology # The very small angle neutron scattering instrument at the National Institute of Standards and Technology A description and the performance of the very small angle neutron scattering (VSANS) diffractometer at the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) are presented. The instrument is designed to bridge the gap between traditional SANS and ultra-SANS (USANS) by providing a measurement range of momentum transfer $q$ from $2 \times 10^{-4}$ to $0.7\ \text{Å}^{-1}$. ## Instrument Overview The VSANS instrument allows for the simultaneous measurement of the entire scattering angle range from $8 \times 10^{-5}$ to $\pi/6\ \text{rad}$ ($30^\circ$) using three separate detector carriages on rails holding nine 2D detector arrays. ### Technical Specifications Summary | Feature | Specification | | :--- | :--- | | **Source** | $m = 1$ straight neutron guide ($60 \times 150\ \text{mm}$ cross section), filter: $200\ \text{mm Be} + 200\ \text{mm Bi}$ | | **Monochromators** | (i) HOPG ($\Delta\lambda/\lambda = 0.8\text{--}1.0\%$, $4.75 < \lambda < 5.5\ \text{Å}$)
(ii) Neutron velocity selector (NVS) ($\Delta\lambda/\lambda = 12.5\%$, $4.5 < \lambda < 20.0\ \text{Å}$)
(iii) Deflector ($\Delta\lambda/\lambda = 44\%$, $\lambda = 5.34\ \text{Å}$)
(iv) Filter only ($\Delta\lambda/\lambda = 80\%$, $\lambda = 6.20\ \text{Å}$) | | **Source–Sample Distance** | Nominally $6\text{--}24\ \text{m}$ (in $2\ \text{m}$ steps via guide segments) | | **Sample–Detector Distance** | (i) Rear: $11.03\text{--}23.65\ \text{m}$
(ii) Middle: $2.85\text{--}20.87\ \text{m}$
(iii) Front: $0.84\text{--}10.14\ \text{m}$ | | **Collimation** | (i) Standard circular pinhole
(ii) 18 Converging-Beam (CB) with $\text{MgF}_2$ lenses and prisms
(iii) Slits (width $1\text{--}60\ \text{mm}$, height $1\text{--}150\ \text{mm}$) | | **Sample Area** | $2\ \text{m}$-long area with four-axis stage and vacuum pipe | | **$q$ range** | $2 \times 10^{-4}$ to $0.7\ \text{Å}^{-1}$ | | **Detectors** | (i) Rear: $^6\text{LiF ZnS(Ag)}$ scintillator with CCD ($0.85\ \text{mm}$ resolution)
(ii) Middle/Front: $8\ \text{mm}$-diameter $^3\text{He}$ tubes (48 tube panels) | | **Polarization** | Double-V polarizer ($99\%$), RF spin flipper ($> 99.99\%$), $^3\text{He}$ spin analyzer |

text:
iucr_the_very_small_angle_neutron_scattering_instrument_at_the_national_institute_of_standards_and_technology > Key Technical Features / Source and Beam Optics

# The very small angle neutron scattering instrument at the National Institute of Standards and Technology

## Key Technical Features

### 1. Momentum Transfer and Resolution
The $q$ range is determined by the relation $q \simeq 2\pi\theta/\lambda$. High $q$ resolution is achieved via multiple converging-beam collimation with circular pinholes, refractive lenses, and prisms. For experiments requiring higher beam intensity over relaxed vertical resolution, narrow slit collimation is used.

### 2. Wavelength Selection Options
The instrument provides four automated options for wavelength selection:
*   **Neutron Velocity Selector (NVS):** Provides a triangular distribution with $\Delta\lambda/\lambda = 12.5\%$.
*   **Supermirror Deflection Filter:** A cutoff filter reflecting $\lambda > 8.3\ \text{Å}$, combined with a Be filter cutting $\lambda < 4\ \text{Å}$, resulting in $\Delta\lambda/\lambda \simeq 44\%$.
*   **HOPG Double Monochromator:** Two reflections of Highly Oriented Pyrolytic Graphite crystals provide high resolution ($\Delta\lambda/\lambda = 0.8\text{--}1.0\%$).
*   **Filter Only:** Utilizes the full transmission of the Be–Bi beam filter.

### 3. Detector Configuration
To cover a wide angular range without time offsets, the instrument uses three carriages:
*   **Rear Carriage:** High-resolution 2D detector.
*   **Front and Middle Carriages:** Four 2D tube detectors that can be arranged into a "picture frame" of adjustable size, allowing the inner unscattered beam to pass to downstream detectors.

### 4. Polarization Analysis
Full polarization analysis is available using:
*   A high-performance supermirror polarizer.
*   An RF spin flipper (flipping ratios $> 100$).
*   A high-efficiency $^3\text{He}$ polarization analyzer.

## Source and Beam Optics
The instrument is located on the NG-3 guide ($60 \times 150\ \text{mm}$, $m=1.18$). To minimize background, a Be–Bi filter (cooled to $77\ \text{K}$) is used to attenuate core gamma rays and fast neutrons. The entire instrument is rotated by $0.3^\circ$ to ensure the farthest detector is not in the direct line of sight of the source.

text:
10m_sans_small_angle_neutron_scattering > Overview

# 10m SANS - Small Angle Neutron Scattering

# 10m SANS - Small Angle Neutron Scattering

The 10 meter Small Angle Neutron Scattering (10m SANS) instrument is designed to measure correlations in spatial structure within solid and liquid materials. The technique measures deflections of a neutron beam as it passes through a sample of interest. The spectrum of deflected neutrons reveals important details such as:

*   **Material State:** Amorphous, crystalline, semi-crystalline, liquid crystalline, or combinations thereof.
*   **Composition Distribution:** Relative location of structures (e.g., water relative to a membrane pore, or a surfactant relative to a protein in a biopharmaceutical).
*   **Structural Evolution:** How structure changes with processing parameters critical to end applications.

The instrument provides ample space for sample environments, allowing the installation of equipment such as rheometers to measure the response of a fluid under shear while concurrently collecting SANS data. This allows structural observations to be connected to manufacturing processes including shear, high pressure, high temperature, and environmental exposure. SANS is a foundational technique for developing structure-property-performance relationships in solid materials, liquids, and mixtures.

10m_sans_small_angle_neutron_scattering > Operational Model / Specifications/Capabilities / Instrument Scientists

# 10m SANS - Small Angle Neutron Scattering

## Operational Model
The 10m SANS is constructed similarly to the two 30 meter SANS instruments at the NCNR, with the primary difference being the overall maximum length (10 meters vs. 30 meters). 

The 10m SANS is operated by the **NIST nSoft consortium** ([www.nist.gov/nsoft](http://www.nist.gov/nsoft)), which partners NIST scientists with staff from member companies. Activities on the 10m SANS are performed for the benefit of the consortium, including:
*   Experiments performed with member companies.
*   Measurement development activities that provide new or enhanced capabilities for members.

Users wishing to access the 10m SANS are encouraged to contact the instrument scientists.

## Specifications/Capabilities

| Parameter | Specification |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Q-Range** | $0.03\text{ nm}^{-1}$ to $6.0\text{ nm}^{-1}$ |
| **Size Regime** | $1\text{ nm}$ to $200\text{ nm}$ |
| **Source** | Neutron Guide (NG-B), cross-section: $60\text{ mm} \times 60\text{ mm}$ |
| **Monochromator** | Mechanical velocity selector with variable speed and pitch |
| **Wavelength Range** | $4.0\text{ \AA}$ to $20.0\text{ \AA}$ |
| **Wavelength Resolution** | $9\%$ to $30\% \Delta\lambda/\lambda$ (FWHM) |
| **Source-to-Sample Distance** | $3.0\text{ m}$ to $1\text{ m}$ via insertion of guide sections |
| **Collimation** | Circular pinhole collimation |
| **Sample Size** | $0\text{ mm}$ to $25\text{ mm}$ diameter |
| **Sample-to-Detector Distance** | $1.3\text{ m}$ to $5.2\text{ m}$ continuously variable |
| **Detector** | $640\text{ mm} \times 640\text{ mm}$ $^3\text{He}$ position-sensitive proportional counter with a $5.08\text{ mm} \times 5.08\text{ mm}$ resolution |

## Instrument Scientists
*   Kathleen Weigandt: [contact details omitted]
*   Ronald L. Jones: [contact details omitted]
*   Tyler Martin: [contact details omitted]
*   Paul Butler: [contact details omitted]

text:
vsans_overview > Instrument characteristics / Detector system

# VSANS — Very Small-Angle Neutron Scattering Diffractometer Overview

## Instrument characteristics

| Parameter | Value / description |
|---|---|
| Source guide | 60 mm wide x 150 mm tall |
| Wavelength range | 4 Å to 20 Å |
| Wavelength resolution | 2% graphite, 12.5% selector, or white beam over 4 Å <= wavelength <= 8 Å |
| Source-to-sample distance | 4 m to 22 m, in 2 m steps |
| Sample-to-detector distance | 0.6 m to 22.5 m, continuous |
| Collimation | circular pinholes; rectangular XY slits; 18 converging circular beams with lens and prism; 3 converging narrow rectangular beams with lens |
| Sample size | circular 1 mm to 30 mm diameter; rectangular width 1 mm to 18 mm and height 12 mm to 75 mm; converging beams typically 35 mm x 72 mm |
| Q-range | about 2e-4 Å^-1 to 1.0 Å^-1 in one measurement |
| Detectors | 1.2 mm FWHM 2D detector, 150 mm x 450 mm; 8 mm FWHM 2D tube detector with four 384 mm x 1000 mm panels; 8 mm FWHM 2D tube detector with four 384 mm x 500 mm panels |

## Detector system

The detector vessel contains three movable detector carriages. The front and middle carriages use 8 mm resolution detector panels. The back carriage uses a 1 mm resolution Anger camera.

Moveable 2D detector panels form a picture-frame arrangement:

- side panels: 384 mm x 1000 mm;
- top and bottom panels: 500 mm x 384 mm;
- one layer of 8 mm diameter helium-3 tubes.

This detector arrangement is described as extending q-range by a factor of 30. The presentation compares the concept to other multiple-carriage instruments, including D33 at ILL Grenoble and BILBY at ANSTO.

The high-resolution detector procurement slide describes an SNS-type Anger camera of 15 cm x 45 cm, with 1 mm-class position resolution. It also notes that the instrument was rotated 0.3 degrees to avoid reactor-core gamma rays.

vsans_overview > Collimation and q-range modes / Count-rate and flux rationale

# VSANS — Very Small-Angle Neutron Scattering Diffractometer Overview

## Collimation and q-range modes

The presentation highlights three main collimation options for extending the q-range from about 2e-4 Å^-1 to about 1 Å^-1 in one measurement:

| Mode | Source aperture | Sample aperture | Sample-to-detector | Wavelength | Approximate qmin | Approximate qmax | Beam current |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Narrow slit | 5 mm x 150 mm | 2.5 mm x 75 mm | 22.5 m | 6 Å | 2.3e-4 Å^-1 | 0.45 Å^-1 | 9.7e4 s^-1 |
| Converging beams | 6 mm diameter | 35 mm x 72 mm, with 10 mm diameter beams | 22.5 m | 7.5 Å | 1.9e-4 Å^-1 | 0.36 Å^-1 | 9.0e3 s^-1 |
| Large pinhole | 60 mm diameter | 30 mm diameter | 22.5 m | 6 Å | 2.8e-3 Å^-1 | 0.45 Å^-1 | 1.4e6 s^-1 |

The front and middle detector carriages use four panels each. The back carriage uses an approximately 150 mm wide x 450 mm tall Anger camera.

## Count-rate and flux rationale

The presentation argues that VSANS can offer large measurement-rate gains compared with USANS-like approaches. For an example comparison using a lens configuration with sample-to-detector distance 15 m and wavelength 8.1 Å, the presentation states that SANS with lenses can obtain the same statistics about 10,000 times faster than USANS under the stated assumptions of equal sample thickness, transmission, and 8 mm radius sample aperture.

For VSANS at qmin = 2e-4 Å^-1, the presentation estimates:

- narrow-slit collimation: up to 100,000 times faster;
- converging-beam collimation: up to 1,000 times faster.

The broader flux argument is that neutron sources have much lower brilliance than some X-ray synchrotron sources, so VSANS aims to recover usable count rate by using larger samples, larger wavelength bandwidth, larger detector solid angle, and multiple collimation options.

text:
vsans_overview > Sample area and cells / Polarization and automated optics / Capability summary / Source-cleaning notes

# VSANS — Very Small-Angle Neutron Scattering Diffractometer Overview

## Sample area and cells

The presentation emphasizes larger sample-area capability and a new VSANS sample area without a sample chamber. The sample table has about 1.5 m travel, and the new sample area is described as about 2 m.

Example liquid-cell areas listed in the presentation include:

| Cell type | Dimensions | Approximate area |
|---|---:|---:|
| Current Ti cell | 19 mm diameter | 284 mm^2 |
| Medium Ti cell | 28 mm diameter | 616 mm^2 |
| Helma cell 404 | 18.5 mm x 38 mm | 703 mm^2 |
| Large Ti cell | 40 mm diameter | 1260 mm^2 |
| Custom quartz cell | 35 mm x 72 mm | 2500 mm^2 |

## Polarization and automated optics

The new capabilities summary lists automated optics including:

- graphite monochromator with Δλ/λ = 2%;
- double-V polarizer with P > 99%, used with RF flipper and helium-3 analyzer.

## Capability summary

The presentation summarizes VSANS capabilities as:

- factor-of-four smaller q enabled by the high-resolution 1 mm detector;
- higher beam current through converging beams and larger sample sizes;
- narrow-slit and white-beam modes for higher count rate, with additional smearing;
- extended q-range through three independent detector carriages, with qmax/qmin = 2,000;
- larger sample area of about 2 m;
- automated optics for monochromation and polarization.

The final prediction is that many experiments with weak scattering or small samples would choose narrow-slit or white-beam operation to increase count rate, but that signal-to-noise would not necessarily improve.

## Source-cleaning notes

This Markdown file condenses a 24-page presentation PDF into a text-first instrument overview. Slide imagery, decorative drawings, page-layout artifacts, and repeated visual labels were removed. Instrument specifications, q-range and detector details, collimation modes, count-rate arguments, sample-area details, polarization notes, and the final capabilities summary were preserved. Personal author names from the title slide were retained because they are part of the static presentation citation context; no live contact details were present in the source PDF.
